preamble: The shift from the voiceless alveolar stop [t] to a rhotic sound [r] (specifically the alveolar tap [ɾ]) is a widespread form of lenition known as flapping. In intervocalic positions, the stop closure is shortened and voiced, resulting in a sound that is phonetically a rhotic tap.

examples.note: The /t/ is realized as a voiced tap, which is the same sound as the Spanish single 'r'.
Systematic flapping of intervocalic alveolar stops.

In Enga (spoken in Enga Province, Papua New Guinea), the voiceless alveolar stop /t/ is regularly realized as the alveolar tap or trill [r] in intervocalic position. This allophonic rule is systematic and well-documented in the SIL phonology description of Enga.
